heroOverview: Diplopia is the simultaneous perception of two images of a single object, arising either from misalignment of the two eyes (binocular diplopia) or from an optical disturbance within one eye (monocular diplopia). Binocular diplopia, which resolves when one eye is closed, signals a problem with ocular muscle control, the cranial nerves that move the eyes, or the brainstem, and always requires prompt medical evaluation to rule out a life-threatening cause. Monocular diplopia — persistent even with one eye shut — typically originates in the cornea or lens and is far less urgent. The most common serious causes include third, fourth, and sixth cranial nerve palsies, myasthenia gravis, thyroid eye disease, and orbital fracture. Population studies suggest diplopia accounts for roughly 1.8% of neuro-ophthalmological referrals, but its significance is disproportionate to its frequency: new binocular diplopia in an adult over 40 demands same-day neurological exclusion of stroke, aneurysm, or expanding mass.

keyStats.prevalence: Binocular diplopia accounts for approximately 1.8% of all neuro-ophthalmology referrals; true population prevalence is difficult to estimate because most serious causes are evaluated under the primary diagnosis
keyStats.demographics: Occurs across all ages; cranial nerve palsies predominate after age 50, while childhood strabismus and accommodative esotropia cause diplopia in younger patients
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; vascular cranial nerve palsies cluster in patients over 55 with hypertension or diabetes; myasthenia gravis presents bimodally at ages 20-30 (women) and over 60 (men)
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global incidence not well established; stroke-related diplopia contributes to approximately 10-15% of acute stroke presentations

definition: Diplopia (ICD-10: H53.2) is the visual perception of two images of a single object. The critical diagnostic distinction is between binocular diplopia — present when both eyes are open and eliminated by covering either eye — and monocular diplopia, which persists even with the unaffected eye covered and can therefore not result from eye misalignment. Binocular diplopia results from any condition that disrupts the precise alignment of the visual axes, preventing the two foveae from fixing simultaneously on the same point. The brain, normally suppressing or fusing slight discrepancies, cannot reconcile the images when separation is large, producing the subjective double vision. The six extraocular muscles of each eye — the medial and lateral recti, the superior and inferior recti, and the superior and inferior obliques — are innervated by three cranial nerves: the oculomotor (CN III), trochlear (CN IV), and abducens (CN VI). Dysfunction of any of these nerves, or of the muscles themselves or their mechanical supports, produces characteristic patterns of diplopia that a trained examiner uses to localise the lesion. Monocular diplopia, by contrast, arises from optical aberrations within a single eye, most commonly corneal irregularity (keratoconus, pterygia, or corneal scar), lens changes (nuclear sclerosis, astigmatism, or early posterior capsule opacification after cataract surgery), or rarely retinal disease. The urgency of investigation depends heavily on the acuity of onset: new-onset binocular diplopia, especially with associated headache, ptosis, or pupil change, requires same-day neuroimaging to exclude aneurysm, stroke, or an expanding mass in the posterior fossa or cavernous sinus.

typesClassification.description: The most clinically important form; both eyes are open for the double image to occur, and covering either eye immediately eliminates it. Caused by ocular misalignment (strabismus) from cranial nerve palsy, extraocular muscle restriction, or neuromuscular junction disease.

Double vision persists when the affected eye alone is open and the other is covered. Caused by optical distortion within the eye itself — corneal irregularity, lens changes, severe astigmatism, or early cataract. Generally less urgent than binocular diplopia.
Images are displaced above and below each other, strongly suggesting trochlear (CN IV) nerve palsy or inferior rectus restriction (e.g., orbital floor fracture or thyroid eye disease).
Images are side by side; most commonly caused by abducens (CN VI) nerve palsy producing esotropia (eyes turned inward) or by medial rectus restriction.
Images are tilted relative to each other, characteristically seen with superior oblique palsy from CN IV dysfunction, in which the patient tilts the head to compensate.

primarySymptoms: Seeing two separate or overlapping images of a single object — the cardinal symptom — which may be horizontal, vertical, oblique, or tilted depending on which muscle or nerve is affected.
Resolution of double vision immediately upon closing or covering one eye (in binocular diplopia), confirming the cause lies in eye alignment rather than within one eye.
Involuntary head tilt or turn adopted to move the eyes into a gaze direction that minimises or eliminates double vision (compensatory head posture).
Drooping of one eyelid (ptosis), which may accompany third cranial nerve palsy and can partly occlude vision in the affected eye, masking the diplopia.
A widened or unreactive pupil on the same side as the ptosis and eye movement limitation, suggesting a compressive third-nerve palsy requiring urgent imaging.
Eyestrain, frontal headache, or nausea from the effort of trying to suppress the second image or maintain single vision.
Difficulty with depth perception and three-dimensional tasks such as pouring liquids, descending stairs, or reading, as binocular stereo vision is disrupted.
Squinting one eye voluntarily to eliminate the second image and restore useful single vision.
Blurred rather than clearly doubled vision when the image separation is small, as in mild or recovering palsies.
Worsening double vision toward the end of the day or after sustained near work, characteristic of myasthenia gravis, which produces fatigable weakness.
earlyWarningSigns: Intermittent or momentary double vision — even if it resolves quickly — should always be reported to a clinician, as it may represent transient ischaemia.
A slight sense that a print line is doubled or shadowed during reading, particularly if this is new and persistent.
Mild difficulty reading, threading a needle, or catching objects, suggesting subtle misalignment before frank diplopia is noticed.
A family member noticing that one eye appears slightly turned or that the eyelid is drooping on one side.
emergencySigns: New diplopia with severe headache, especially the 'worst headache of my life', which may indicate a ruptured or expanding intracranial aneurysm compressing CN III.

Diplopia with a unilateral dilated and unreactive pupil and eyelid ptosis — the classic presentation of a posterior communicating artery aneurysm — requiring immediate vascular neurosurgery assessment.
Sudden-onset double vision in a patient with vascular risk factors, particularly if accompanied by facial weakness, arm or leg weakness, or speech difficulty, indicating stroke.
Diplopia with fever, orbital pain, restricted eye movement, and a red swollen eye, suggesting orbital cellulitis or cavernous sinus thrombosis.
Any new binocular diplopia without a clear, previously established benign cause warrants same-day medical evaluation — do not self-treat or wait.

causes.description: The most common isolated ocular motor nerve palsy; lateral rectus muscle weakness causes the affected eye to turn inward, producing horizontal diplopia that worsens on gaze toward the affected side. Vascular ischaemia (hypertension, diabetes) accounts for the majority in adults over 50; raised intracranial pressure, tumour, or demyelination must be excluded.

Third-nerve palsy impairs all movements except lateral gaze and downward rotation, causing the eye to deviate outward and downward with ptosis. A pupil-involving CN III palsy — the pupil is dilated and fixed — indicates posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proved otherwise and is a neurological emergency requiring immediate imaging.

The superior oblique muscle fails, producing vertical and torsional diplopia worse on downgaze, typically noticed when reading or descending stairs. CN IV palsy is often congenital or post-traumatic; the patient characteristically tilts the head to the opposite shoulder to minimise torsion.

Autoimmune inflammation causes swelling and fibrosis of the extraocular muscles — most commonly the inferior rectus — which become restricted rather than weak. Vertical diplopia on upgaze is characteristic. Thyroid eye disease may occur with normal thyroid function but is most common in Graves hyperthyroidism.

Autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction produce fatigable ocular muscle weakness; ptosis and diplopia that worsen with sustained eye movement or toward evening are the hallmarks. Ocular myasthenia remains purely ocular in 50% of cases and generalises in the rest within 2-3 years.

Blunt trauma to the orbit drives the globe backward, fracturing the thin orbital floor, and the inferior rectus and inferior oblique muscles or surrounding fat can become trapped in the defect, limiting upward gaze and causing vertical diplopia.

Infarcts or tumours affecting the brainstem nuclei, fascicles, or the nerves before they exit the skull base produce diplopia, often with additional neurological findings (vertigo, ataxia, dysarthria, hemiparesis). Any new diplopia without a clear peripheral cause must be evaluated with neuroimaging.

Adults who were treated for, or silently compensated for, childhood strabismus can experience breakdown of fusion under physical or psychological stress, resulting in binocular diplopia from previously managed misalignment.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 50 with hypertension or diabetes
riskFactors.description: Vascular ischaemia of the small vessels supplying CN III, IV, or VI is the most common cause of isolated cranial nerve palsy in older adults with cardiovascular risk factors; risk is reduced by blood pressure and glycaemic control.
Autoimmune neuromuscular or orbital inflammation is a frequent cause of ocular motility disorders; the underlying autoimmune predisposition cannot be modified but the disease can be treated.
Blow-out orbital fractures and direct cranial nerve injuries from head trauma are common causes of diplopia in younger patients.

riskFactors.factor: Intracranial aneurysm or raised intracranial pressure
riskFactors.description: Expanding aneurysms compress CN III; hydrocephalus and mass lesions stretch CN VI over the petrous bone. Uncontrolled hypertension raises aneurysm rupture risk.
Demyelination of cranial nerve fascicles in the brainstem is a common cause of diplopia in younger adults, with internuclear ophthalmoplegia being the characteristic pattern.

riskFactors.factor: Prior strabismus surgery or history of childhood squint
riskFactors.description: A history of ocular misalignment in childhood predisposes to decompensation of binocular fusion later in life, particularly during illness or extreme fatigue.
Monocular diplopia frequently follows cataract surgery if residual astigmatism, posterior capsule opacification, or multifocal lens halos create a split image within the operated eye.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 50 with cardiovascular risk factors, in whom ischaemic cranial nerve palsy predominates
Young adults aged 20-40 with multiple sclerosis, where brainstem demyelination causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia
Women aged 20-40 with myasthenia gravis; men over 60 represent the second peak of myasthenia incidence
Patients with known Graves thyroid disease, in whom orbital inflammation develops in approximately 25-50%
Children and young adults with decompensating congenital strabismus
Any patient following head or orbital trauma regardless of age
diagnosisOverview: Evaluation of diplopia begins with a careful history: onset (sudden vs. gradual), pattern (constant vs. intermittent), direction of maximum separation (horizontal, vertical, torsional), associated symptoms (headache, ptosis, pupil change, facial numbness, arm or leg symptoms), and prior ocular or neurological history. The cover test distinguishes binocular from monocular diplopia: covering each eye alternately reveals whether the double image resolves with occlusion of one eye (binocular) or persists with either eye covered (monocular). If binocular, the examiner assesses eye movements in the nine positions of gaze with a penlight or cover test to identify the restricted or paretic muscle. Diplopia charting (Hess screen or Lancaster red-green test) quantifies the degree of deviation across the gaze field. The pupil is examined carefully — a dilated, poorly reactive pupil with a CN III pattern demands immediate neuroimaging. Ptosis, the levator function, and the fatigue test (prolonged upgaze for 1-2 minutes to elicit ptosis worsening) are evaluated for myasthenia gravis. Proptosis, lid retraction, and conjunctival injection suggest thyroid eye disease. Slit-lamp examination is performed for monocular diplopia to look for corneal irregularity or lens abnormality. Orbital CT is the preferred imaging for suspected fracture or thyroid eye disease. Brain MRI with dedicated cranial nerve sequences and MRA of the circle of Willis are indicated for any unexplained binocular diplopia, especially with a pupil abnormality or additional neurological findings. Blood tests include acetylcholine receptor antibody titres (myasthenia), thyroid function and TSH receptor antibody (thyroid eye disease), inflammatory markers, and fasting glucose.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The fundamental clinical test to determine whether diplopia is binocular (eliminated by covering one eye) or monocular (persists with one eye covered), and to measure the angle of ocular deviation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner alternately covers each eye while you fix on a target. You will be asked whether the image doubles when both eyes are open and whether it disappears when an eye is covered. No drops or instruments touch the eye.

diagnosticTests.test: Ocular motility testing and Hess/Lancaster chart
diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the deficit in eye movements across the nine cardinal gaze positions to identify the weak or restricted muscle and its innervating cranial nerve, guiding localisation of the lesion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You wear special red-green glasses and follow a light on a grid on the wall. The examiner records your responses to map the pattern of restricted movement. Takes approximately 15-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Pupil examination and swinging flashlight test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects a dilated, fixed pupil suggesting compressive CN III palsy (aneurysm), a relative afferent pupillary defect from optic nerve disease, or a pharmacologically dilated pupil.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small torch is moved between your eyes while you look at a distant target. The examiner observes both pupils for size, symmetry, and reactivity. Takes about 2 minutes and requires no instruments.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI with cranial nerve sequences and MR angiography
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies stroke, demyelination, posterior fossa mass, or an intracranial aneurysm compressing the CN III, particularly when pupil involvement is present or cause is unexplained.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in a magnetic resonance scanner for 30-60 minutes. No radiation is used. An intravenous contrast agent (gadolinium) may be injected. MRA assesses the cerebral arteries without an invasive procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for suspected orbital floor fracture, showing bone integrity and soft-tissue entrapment, or for thyroid eye disease, demonstrating enlarged extraocular muscles.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scanner takes thin-slice images of the orbits in about 5-10 minutes. Radiation dose is low. No contrast is usually required for fracture assessment.

diagnosticTests.test: Acetylcholine receptor antibody and ice pack test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies confirm autoimmune myasthenia gravis; the ice pack test (applying ice to the eyelid for 2 minutes and documenting ptosis improvement) provides a rapid bedside diagnostic screen for myasthenia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw for the AChR antibody takes a few minutes. The ice pack test is non-invasive and takes about 5 minutes. A result is available within days for serology.

diagnosticTests.test: Thyroid function tests and TSH receptor antibody
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Graves hyperthyroidism and its orbital autoimmune process (thyroid eye disease) as a cause of restricted ocular motility and diplopia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results usually available within 1-2 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of diplopia is directed at the underlying cause rather than at the symptom itself. For ischaemic cranial nerve palsies — the most common cause in older adults — vascular risk factor management (blood pressure, glucose, lipids, smoking cessation) is the primary intervention, with 80-90% of isolated microvascular palsies resolving spontaneously within 3-6 months. Prism spectacles reduce the angle between the two images by bending light, effectively aligning the double images without affecting the underlying condition; they are highly practical for stable or slowly improving diplopia. A temporary stick-on Fresnel prism can be applied to an existing spectacle lens while awaiting spontaneous recovery. Patching one eye eliminates double vision immediately and is appropriate as short-term relief, but prolonged patching of an adult should be avoided to prevent amblyopia in the non-dominant eye. For myasthenia gravis, acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) improve neuromuscular transmission; thymectomy, immunosuppressants, or biological agents are used for generalised or refractory disease. Thyroid eye disease is managed in a step-wise fashion: selenium supplementation and smoking cessation for mild-to-moderate active disease, intravenous methylprednisolone for active moderate-to-severe inflammation, and orbital decompression or strabismus surgery in the inactive, fibrotic phase. Orbital floor fractures with muscle entrapment require surgical repair within days to weeks to maximise recovery of motility. Aneurysmal CN III palsy requires urgent vascular neurosurgical or interventional neuroradiology assessment for coil embolisation or clipping. Strabismus surgery — recession or resection of individual extraocular muscles — is the definitive treatment for stable, non-progressive diplopia from strabismus or the residual phase of a resolved palsy.

medicalTreatments.name: Fresnel or ground-in prism spectacles
medicalTreatments.description: Prism lenses bend light entering one eye so that both images fall on corresponding retinal points simultaneously, effectively merging the double images without addressing the underlying eye movement problem. Fresnel prisms are thin, stick-on plastic films applied to a lens; ground-in prisms are permanent. Used for stable angles of deviation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores single binocular vision in approximately 75-85% of patients with small-to-moderate stable deviations when prism is correctly prescribed.

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin injection (chemodenervation)
medicalTreatments.description: Botulinum toxin A injected into an overacting antagonist muscle (e.g., the medial rectus when the lateral rectus is weak in CN VI palsy) temporarily weakens the muscle to partially restore alignment, preventing contracture and sometimes providing durable improvement after recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In CN VI palsy, approximately 50-60% of patients treated in the acute phase show improved or resolved diplopia compared with untreated cases; effect lasts 3-4 months and can be repeated.

medicalTreatments.name: Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) for myasthenia gravis
medicalTreatments.description: Inhibits the breakdown of acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, improving the signal to ocular muscles and reducing fatigable ptosis and diplopia. First-line symptomatic treatment for ocular and generalised myasthenia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves ptosis and ocular motility in approximately 50-70% of ocular myasthenia patients; responses are partial and treatment of the underlying autoimmune process is needed for durable remission.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous methylprednisolone for thyroid eye disease
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose intravenous corticosteroids given in weekly pulses reduce active orbital inflammation, limiting progression of muscle fibrosis and associated diplopia. Used in the active, inflammatory phase of moderate-to-severe Graves orbitopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces clinical activity score by 2 or more points in approximately 60-70% of patients with active moderate-to-severe thyroid eye disease in randomised controlled trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Occlusion therapy (patching or occlusive lens)
medicalTreatments.description: Covering one eye eliminates the double image immediately and allows the patient to function during evaluation and awaiting recovery or surgery. A frosted or opaque lens in spectacles is cosmetically preferable to a black patch for daytime use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 100% effective at eliminating diplopia symptoms during use; does not treat the underlying condition and prolonged use can interfere with binocular function.

surgicalOptions.name: Strabismus surgery (extraocular muscle recession/resection)
surgicalOptions.description: One or more extraocular muscles are weakened (recession — the muscle insertion is moved backward) or strengthened (resection — a portion is removed and the muscle reattached anteriorly) to align the eyes and restore single vision in primary gaze. Performed under local or general anaesthesia as a day-case procedure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Single surgery achieves alignment within 10 prism dioptres of orthotropia in approximately 70-80% of appropriately selected patients; a second procedure is required in 20-30%.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital floor fracture repair
surgicalOptions.description: Entrapped inferior rectus muscle or fat is released from the fractured floor and a mesh, titanium plate, or absorbable material implant is used to reconstitute the orbital floor. Best results when performed within 2 weeks of injury before muscle fibrosis establishes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Vertical gaze limitation improves in approximately 80-90% of patients when surgery is performed early; persistent restriction occurs in approximately 10-20% of complex fractures.

surgicalOptions.name: Orbital decompression for thyroid eye disease
surgicalOptions.description: One or more orbital walls are surgically removed to increase orbital volume, reduce proptosis, and relieve compressive optic neuropathy. Performed in the quiescent phase after inflammation is controlled. May induce or worsen diplopia as a complication, which is then managed with subsequent strabismus surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Achieves 4-6 mm proptosis reduction in approximately 80% of cases; de-novo diplopia occurs in 10-30% of patients as a known complication, addressed by later strabismus surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Yoked prism glasses for non-concomitant deviations
alternativeTreatments.description: Prism in both lenses shifts the visual field in the same direction, allowing the patient to move the gaze field into a position of single vision without head turning. Used as an adjunct when head posture compensation is impractical.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vision therapy/orthoptic exercises for convergence insufficiency
alternativeTreatments.description: Supervised exercises can strengthen convergence and improve fusional range in patients whose diplopia results from vergence dysfunction rather than muscle palsy, particularly in younger patients and those with mild decompensating strabismus.

whySeeSpecialist: Any new binocular diplopia warrants same-day evaluation by an ophthalmologist or neurologist, given the potential for life-threatening underlying causes including intracranial aneurysm and stroke. A neuro-ophthalmologist offers the most efficient integration of ocular examination findings with neurological localisation. Ophthalmologists manage strabismus surgery, prism prescription, and orbital decompression. Neurologists and neurosurgeons manage intracranial vascular causes, demyelination, and myasthenia. Endocrinologists co-manage Graves thyroid disease. The referral pathway is determined by the suspected diagnosis after the initial emergency causes have been excluded.
doctorSelectionGuide: For unexplained binocular diplopia, a neuro-ophthalmologist with access to dedicated cranial nerve MRI sequences and close neurosurgical liaison is ideal. For clearly stable strabismus-related diplopia requiring prism or surgery, an experienced strabismus surgeon within a paediatric ophthalmology or strabismus-specialist unit can discuss all surgical and optical options. For thyroid eye disease, a multidisciplinary clinic combining ophthalmology, endocrinology, and orbital surgery provides the best coordinated care.
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure, blood glucose, and cholesterol to reduce the risk of microvascular ischaemic cranial nerve palsy, the most preventable common cause of diplopia in adults over 50.
Wear appropriate eye protection during sports and activities that carry a risk of orbital or facial trauma.
Seek early treatment for Graves hyperthyroidism and stop smoking — smoking doubles the risk of thyroid eye disease and worsens its severity.
Report intermittent double vision promptly; early detection of myasthenia gravis or an aneurysm improves outcomes.
Adults with a childhood squint history should advise their eye-care provider so that early decompensation is detected and managed.
lifestyleModifications: If wearing prism glasses, ensure the prescription is reviewed whenever the angle of deviation changes, as the prism requirement often shifts during recovery from a palsy.
Avoid activities requiring precise depth perception (driving, operating machinery) until diplopia is adequately controlled with prism or resolved.
Maintain head-posture compensation when wearing prism is not possible, and use adequate lighting to maximise image clarity.
Patients with myasthenia gravis should avoid overheating, extreme fatigue, and medications known to worsen neuromuscular transmission (aminoglycoside antibiotics, quinidine, chloroquine).
Keep regular follow-up appointments to track recovery — delayed recovery beyond 6 months for an ischaemic palsy warrants further investigation.

recommended: A heart-healthy diet low in saturated fat and sodium to support vascular risk factor management
Antioxidant-rich vegetables and fruits to support general ocular and vascular health
Selenium supplementation (100 µg twice daily) has evidence for mild-to-moderate active thyroid eye disease in European RCTs
avoid: Smoking — directly worsens thyroid eye disease activity and severity and is a cardiovascular risk factor
Excess alcohol during periods of acute diplopia as it worsens gaze stability and may confound neurological assessment

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is appropriate and encouraged for overall cardiovascular health and vascular risk factor control. During an acute episode of new diplopia, avoid driving or using heavy machinery until the cause is established and vision is stable. Contact sports and activities with significant fall risk (cycling, ladders, team sports) should be modified or paused until reliable single vision is restored, as diplopia significantly impairs depth perception and reaction time.
dailyManagement: Wear prescribed prism correction consistently — removing glasses in environments where double vision persists increases fall and injury risk.
Attend all follow-up appointments as scheduled; recovery from microvascular cranial nerve palsy should be assessed every 4-6 weeks.
Keep a log of symptom variability — worsening toward evening strongly suggests myasthenia gravis and should be reported.
Report any new associated symptoms (headache, facial numbness, limb weakness) immediately as they can signal worsening neurological disease.
Discuss driving safety with your clinician; many jurisdictions require documentation that diplopia is controlled before driving can resume.
prognosis: The prognosis for diplopia depends heavily on its cause. Isolated microvascular cranial nerve palsies — CN III, IV, or VI palsies in patients with hypertension or diabetes — carry an excellent prognosis, with spontaneous recovery in 80-90% of cases within 3-6 months. Compressive CN III palsy from an aneurysm has a good outcome if treated surgically within hours to days, but risk of permanent damage or death is substantial if untreated. Myasthenia gravis is a chronic but usually manageable autoimmune disease; remission rates depend on thymectomy and immunotherapy but ocular control is achievable in the majority with treatment. Thyroid eye disease follows a self-limited active phase of approximately 18-24 months followed by stabilisation; persistent diplopia after the inflammatory phase is treated surgically with excellent functional outcomes. Brainstem stroke-related diplopia may partially or completely recover, depending on lesion size and location, with maximum neurological recovery expected within 3-6 months. Decompensated childhood strabismus is well managed surgically with high rates of restored single binocular vision.
recoveryTimeline: Microvascular cranial nerve palsies typically begin improving within 6-12 weeks and fully resolve within 3-6 months in the majority of patients. Strabismus surgery provides immediate structural correction with motility stabilisation over 4-8 weeks. Thyroid eye disease active inflammation resolves over 12-24 months; surgical correction of the resulting strabismus is performed when the angle of deviation has been stable for at least 6 months. Myasthenia gravis management is ongoing, but most patients achieve good symptomatic control within 3-6 months of initiating treatment.
complications: Amblyopia (lazy eye) in children if monocular occlusion or head posture is not managed correctly — one eye may be inadvertently suppressed, reducing best corrected acuity.
Permanent motility restriction if an orbital floor fracture with muscle entrapment is not repaired promptly, resulting in fibrosis and fixed strabismus.
Falls, accidents, and motor vehicle collisions from uncontrolled binocular diplopia, particularly in older adults with balance difficulties.
Missed aneurysm: a pupil-involving CN III palsy attributed to ischaemia rather than compression can lead to catastrophic subarachnoid haemorrhage if imaging is not performed.
Progression of myasthenia gravis from isolated ocular to generalised form with swallowing, breathing, and limb weakness in approximately 50% of patients within 2-3 years.
Depression and reduced quality of life from chronic diplopia that affects driving, reading, and social functioning when underlying causes prove difficult to treat.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Double Vision
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of diplopia causes, diagnosis, and treatment from the AAO.

supportResources.name: NHS — Double Vision
supportResources.description: UK National Health Service guidance on double vision including when to seek urgent care.

supportResources.name: Myasthenia Gravis Foundation of America
supportResources.description: Disease-specific support and education for patients with myasthenia gravis, a common cause of diplopia.

sources.title: Richards BW, Jones FR Jr, Younge BR. Causes and prognosis in 4,278 cases of paralysis of the oculomotor, trochlear, and abducens cranial nerves. American Journal of Ophthalmology, 1992
Biousse V, Newman NJ. Diagnosis and clinical features of common cranial nerve palsies. Seminars in Neurology, 2000
Barton JJ, Fouladvand M. Ocular aspects of myasthenia gravis. Seminars in Neurology, 2000

NHS — Double Vision. National Health Service UK
Crum OM, Kilgore KP, Bhatti MT. Evaluation and treatment of acute diplopia. American Family Physician, 2019

confusedWithConditions.name: Convergence insufficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Convergence insufficiency causes near-distance blur and eyestrain from poor eye-teaming but produces a small intermittent deviation rather than frank constant binocular diplopia; it is confirmed by prism cover test and responds to orthoptic exercises.

confusedWithConditions.name: Esotropia (inward eye turn)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Constant congenital or infantile esotropia causes suppression rather than diplopia because the brain adapts by eliminating the turned eye's image; diplopia from esotropia usually arises from decompensation of an intermittent form or from acquired CN VI palsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia — impaired adduction on lateral gaze with nystagmus in the abducting eye — rather than a simple third, fourth, or sixth nerve palsy; MRI shows demyelinating lesions in the medial longitudinal fasciculus.

relatedConditions.name: Fourth nerve (trochlear) palsy
relatedConditions.relevance: CN IV palsy is the most common isolated cranial nerve cause of vertical diplopia and is a specific form of the general diplopia diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Esotropia
relatedConditions.relevance: Manifest inward eye turn from CN VI palsy or decompensating strabismus is a direct structural cause of horizontal binocular diplopia.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Demyelinating plaques in the brainstem cause internuclear ophthalmoplegia, a characteristic form of binocular diplopia in young adults.

relatedConditions.name: Cranial nerve disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Cranial nerve dysfunction at CN III, IV, or VI is the anatomical substrate for most cases of binocular diplopia.

faqs.question: What is diplopia?
faqs.answer: Diplopia means double vision — seeing two images of a single object. When it occurs with both eyes open but resolves on closing one eye, it is binocular and requires urgent medical evaluation. When it persists even with one eye closed, it is monocular and usually due to a corneal or lens problem in that eye.

faqs.question: Is double vision an emergency?
faqs.answer: New binocular diplopia — double vision that resolves when one eye is covered — should be evaluated the same day. It can signal an intracranial aneurysm, stroke, or other serious neurological cause. Diplopia with severe headache, a dilated pupil, or face/limb weakness is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital attendance.

faqs.question: What causes sudden double vision?
faqs.answer: Sudden-onset binocular diplopia in adults most commonly results from a cranial nerve palsy caused by small-vessel ischaemia (hypertension, diabetes), brainstem stroke, or compressive aneurysm. Myasthenia gravis and thyroid eye disease tend to develop more gradually. Any sudden diplopia warrants same-day medical evaluation.

faqs.question: Can double vision go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, when caused by an ischaemic cranial nerve palsy (a small-vessel blood supply problem to CN III, IV, or VI). Around 80-90% of these palsies resolve spontaneously within 3-6 months with optimal vascular risk factor control. Diplopia from other causes — aneurysm, trauma, thyroid eye disease, myasthenia — requires specific treatment.

faqs.question: Why do I see double with both eyes open but not one?
faqs.answer: This is binocular diplopia, caused by the two eyes pointing in slightly different directions so their images do not land on corresponding retinal points. Closing one eye removes one image entirely, resolving the double vision. The cause is an eye alignment problem from cranial nerve weakness, muscle restriction, or neuromuscular disease.

faqs.question: What is monocular diplopia?
faqs.answer: Monocular diplopia is double or ghost-vision that persists even when only the affected eye is open. It indicates an optical problem within that eye — most commonly corneal irregularity, astigmatism, early cataract, or a problem with an intraocular lens implant — rather than a nerve or alignment problem.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetes is one of the most common causes of ischaemic cranial nerve palsy, particularly CN VI (abducens) palsy producing horizontal diplopia. Good glucose control reduces the risk. Most diabetic cranial nerve palsies recover spontaneously within 3-6 months.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for double vision?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the underlying cause: vascular risk factor control and observation for ischaemic nerve palsies; prism spectacles to align the two images for stable deviations; patching for short-term relief; strabismus surgery for stable misalignment; specific disease treatment for myasthenia gravis, thyroid eye disease, aneurysm, or stroke.

faqs.question: Can glasses fix double vision?
faqs.answer: Prism glasses can reduce or eliminate the double image in many patients by bending light so both images align on the fovea simultaneously. They do not treat the underlying cause but provide functional single vision while recovery occurs or while awaiting surgery. Fresnel prisms are a low-cost temporary option that can be applied to existing spectacles.

faqs.question: Can stress or tiredness cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Extreme fatigue can cause temporary breakdown of binocular fusion in people with latent strabismus, producing intermittent diplopia that resolves with rest. Persistent or frequently recurring diplopia in this context warrants an orthoptic assessment. Myasthenia gravis specifically causes diplopia that worsens with fatigue and toward the end of the day.

faqs.question: Does thyroid disease cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Graves thyroid disease causes an autoimmune inflammation that swells and stiffens the extraocular muscles, typically the inferior rectus, restricting upward gaze and causing vertical diplopia. It can occur even when thyroid hormone levels are normal. Treatment includes anti-inflammatory therapy and, later, strabismus surgery.

faqs.question: Can a brain tumour cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tumours or other masses in the posterior fossa, cavernous sinus, or along the course of the cranial nerves can compress CN III, IV, or VI and produce diplopia. An MRI of the brain and orbits is part of the investigation for any unexplained binocular diplopia that does not have a clear vascular or traumatic cause.

faqs.question: What is the difference between diplopia and blurred vision?
faqs.answer: Blurred vision means a single image that is out of focus; diplopia means two separate or overlapping images. They can coexist when a refractive error causes ghost images, but true binocular diplopia with clear image separation is a distinct symptom pointing to a motility or alignment problem.

faqs.question: Can double vision be a sign of a stroke?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diplopia is present in approximately 10-15% of brainstem strokes. New diplopia with facial weakness, speech difficulty, arm or leg weakness, balance problems, or severe headache is a neurological emergency — call emergency services immediately and note the time of symptom onset for thrombolysis eligibility.

faqs.question: How is the cause of double vision identified?
faqs.answer: A clinician determines the type (binocular vs. monocular), direction (horizontal, vertical, torsional), and associated features (pupil, ptosis, proptosis, neurological signs). This guides targeted investigations: brain MRI for unexplained binocular diplopia, orbital CT for trauma or thyroid disease, and blood tests for myasthenia and thyroid antibodies.

faqs.question: Can migraines cause double vision?
faqs.answer: Hemiplegic migraine and basilar-type migraine can produce transient diplopia as part of the aura. Ophthalmoplegic migraine — recurrent CN III palsy with migraine headache — is rare but recognised. Migraine-related diplopia is typically brief, recurrent, and associated with other migraine features; isolated persistent diplopia without headache is not a migraine phenomenon.

faqs.question: Is diplopia common after cataract surgery?
faqs.answer: Monocular diplopia from ghost or shadow images occurs in some patients after cataract surgery due to posterior capsule opacification, residual refractive error, or optical effects of multifocal intraocular lenses. True binocular strabismus-related diplopia is a less common but recognised complication if local anaesthetic diffuses into the extraocular muscles.

faqs.question: When should I see an eye doctor about double vision?
faqs.answer: Any new binocular double vision — one that resolves when you cover either eye — should be evaluated the same day. A dilated pupil, ptosis, severe headache, or other neurological symptoms require emergency care. Monocular ghosting after cataract surgery is less urgent but should be assessed within days to weeks.

metaDescription: Diplopia means seeing double. Learn causes — stroke, nerve palsy, myasthenia — tests, prism treatment, and when double vision is an emergency. Doctor-reviewed.
